text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY OFFICE VISIT
ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Hematology/Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient, Oncology Follow-Up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 59-year-old male with known metastatic KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma presenting for scheduled follow-up and restaging review after 4 cycles of second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. He is here today to review CT imaging results from earlier this week.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Kowalski is a 59-year-old Polish-American former smoker (20 pack-year history, quit approximately 8 years ago) who was diagnosed with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma approximately 13 months ago. His disease is characterized by a KRAS p.G12D mutation, BRAF and NRAS wild-type status, microsatellite stable (MSS) phenotype, and low tumor mutational burden (6.2 mut/Mb), which precluded the use of anti-EGFR therapy or checkpoint immunotherapy at any point in his treatment course. SMAD4 mutation and PIK3CA E545K were also identified on his initial comprehensive NGS panel.

To briefly summarize his treatment history: he was initiated on first-line mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab approximately 13 months ago, achieving an excellent partial response after 6 and 12 cycles with near-complete response of the segment VII hepatic lesion. He completed 12 cycles of mFOLFOX6 but was transitioned to maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab at that point due to Grade 3 oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy. While on maintenance therapy, his disease remained stable for approximately 2 months, but at the 10-month mark, CT imaging demonstrated progressive disease with new bilateral pulmonary nodules (confirmed to represent metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma on CT-guided biopsy of the right upper lobe nodule - TTF-1 negative, CDX2 positive, CK20 positive), enlargement of the known hepatic metastases in segments II, IV, and VI, growth of the primary sigmoid mass, and new perihepatic lymphadenopathy. His CEA had risen sharply to 94.7 ng/mL at that time.

Given RAS-mutant disease precluding anti-EGFR options, he was transitioned to second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab approximately 2.5 months ago, consistent with the RAISE trial data supporting the addition of ramucirumab to FOLFIRI in the second-line setting. UGT1A1 genotyping prior to initiation revealed *1/*28 heterozygosity (intermediate metabolizer), and he was started at a reduced irinotecan dose of 150 mg/m2 with intention to uptitrate as tolerated. After cycle 2, he developed Grade 2 diarrhea and Grade 2 fatigue, necessitating a further irinotecan dose reduction to 135 mg/m2; this has been maintained through cycles 3 and 4 without escalation. He also developed worsening hypertension (Grade 3) in the setting of ramucirumab (anti-VEGFR2), requiring the addition of hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily to his existing regimen of lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily.

Today's visit is for review of restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed 3 days ago after completion of 4 cycles of FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab.

Since his last visit, Mr. Kowalski reports that his overall functional status is reasonably maintained. He has returned to some light teaching duties but continues to work with reduced hours. His diarrhea has been managed adequately with dietary modification and intermittent loperamide use; he is averaging 2-3 loose stools per day above baseline (improved from prior Grade 2 episode). He denies frank watery diarrhea or incontinence. He reports mild fatigue, particularly in the 5-6 days following each infusion, but notes that he has been recovering in time for the next cycle. He denies fevers, chills, significant nausea, or vomiting. He has noticed some mild thinning of his hair since starting irinotecan but is not bothered by this. His fingertip numbness (residual from oxaliplatin) remains stable and he feels it has marginally improved since stopping oxaliplatin. He denies any worsening exertional dyspnea compared with the last visit, though he notes he does feel more breathless walking up hills than he did a year ago. No hemoptysis. No new rectal bleeding since the sigmoid colectomy has not yet been performed (primary tumor still in situ). Bowel function is relatively preserved; he continues a high-fiber diet and has not required any intervention for obstruction symptoms since initiation of FOLFIRI. He denies significant abdominal pain. Right upper quadrant discomfort is mild and unchanged.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Fatigue as above, moderate, present. No fever or chills. Appetite mildly decreased; weight has been stable over the past 4 weeks (he reports his weight as approximately 172 lbs, down from his baseline of 178 lbs at diagnosis). No significant night sweats.
Respiratory: Mild exertional dyspnea on walking up inclines; no dyspnea at rest. No cough. No hemoptysis.
Gastrointestinal: Loose stools 2-3 per day above baseline, well-managed. No hematochezia currently. No nausea/vomiting. Mild right upper quadrant discomfort, stable. No jaundice, no clay-colored stools.
Musculoskeletal/Neurological: Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy - mild distal fingertip numbness bilaterally, not functionally limiting. Gait stability improved compared with his nadir period.
Cardiovascular: Headaches intermittently, attributed in part to hypertension history - improved with blood pressure optimization. No chest pain or palpitations. No lower extremity edema.
Dermatologic: Grade 1 alopecia (partial thinning). No rash. No hand-foot syndrome.
Genitourinary: No hematuria. Normal urinary function.
All other systems reviewed and negative.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (as previously documented)
1. Stage IV KRAS G12D-mutant sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with bilobar hepatic metastases and pulmonary metastases (confirmed) - current
2. Hypertension - exacerbated by bevacizumab and now ramucirumab; currently on triple antihypertensive therapy
3. Hyperlipidemia - on atorvastatin
4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus - on metformin; HbA1c has remained acceptable; no significant changes in glycemic management during chemotherapy
5. Oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy - residual Grade 1
6. Irinotecan-induced diarrhea - managed with dose reduction and loperamide

SOCIAL HISTORY
High school history teacher, working reduced hours during active treatment. Former smoker, 20 pack-years, quit approximately 8 years prior to diagnosis. Married, with two adult children who are supportive and attend appointments. Denies current alcohol or illicit drug use. Lives in his own home; functional for all ADLs.

FAMILY HISTORY
No family history of colorectal cancer. No known hereditary cancer syndromes in the family. Father had a history of ischemic heart disease. Mother had type 2 diabetes mellitus.

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies. No prior allergic or hypersensitivity reactions to chemotherapy agents administered to date.

MEDICATIONS
1. Irinotecan 135 mg/m2 IV every 14 days (cycle chemotherapy, reduced dose)
2. Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV every 14 days (cycle chemotherapy)
3. 5-Fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus + 2400 mg/m2 IV over 46 hours every 14 days (cycle chemotherapy)
4. Ramucirumab 8 mg/kg IV every 14 days (cycle targeted therapy)
5. Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily
6. Amlodipine 10 mg PO daily
7. Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg PO daily
8. Atorvastatin 20 mg PO daily
9. Metformin 1000 mg PO twice daily
10. Duloxetine 60 mg PO daily (for neuropathic pain management, initiated during oxaliplatin course)
11. Loperamide 2 mg PO as needed for diarrhea (up to 8 mg/day; instructed on rescue dosing)
12. Ondansetron 8 mg PO/SL as needed for nausea
13. Dexamethasone 4 mg PO the evening before and morning of infusion days (premedication per protocol)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: Temperature 36.9 degrees C. Blood pressure 146/94 mmHg (left arm, seated; second reading 144/92 mmHg). Heart rate 78 bpm, regular. Respiratory rate 16. Oxygen saturation 96% on room air. Weight 172.4 lbs (78.2 kg). BSA approximately 1.91 m2. ECOG Performance Status: 1.

General: Mr. Kowalski is a pleasant, well-appearing, middle-aged male in no acute distress. He appears mildly fatigued but is in good spirits today. He is alert and fully oriented. He arrived accompanied by his wife.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Anicteric sclerae. Oropharynx without mucositis or ulceration. Mild patchy alopecia noted on scalp consistent with Grade 1 irinotecan-associated hair thinning.

Neck: Supple. No cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular lymphadenopathy appreciated.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension. No lower extremity pitting edema bilaterally.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or crackles. Breathing is comfortable and unlabored at rest.

Abdominal: Soft, mildly tender to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant and epigastrium. No involuntary guarding or rebound tenderness. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciable on clinical exam (though imaging demonstrates hepatic metastases). Bowel sounds normal and active. No ascites clinically.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x 4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. Mild symmetric diminished sensation to light touch at the distal fingertips bilaterally (consistent with residual Grade 1 oxaliplatin neuropathy); no deficits in feet or ankles. Gait is normal and steady.

Extremities: No clubbing or cyanosis. Warm and well-perfused. No peripheral edema.

Skin: No rash, no erythema, no hand-foot syndrome. Grade 1 alopecia as noted above. No jaundice.

Port/Access Site: Port-a-cath in place, left chest wall; site clean, dry, intact without signs of infection or thrombosis.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (from today's draw and from infusion visit 3 days ago)
CBC: WBC 4.1 x10^9/L (L normal 4.0-11.0), Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (mild anemia, stable from prior), Platelets 198 x10^9/L, ANC 1.8 x10^9/L (adequate for chemotherapy administration).
CMP: Sodium 139, Potassium 3.6, Chloride 103, Bicarbonate 25, BUN 18, Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL, eGFR 74 mL/min/1.73m2 (stable), ALT 42 U/L (mildly elevated, stable), AST 38 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 128 U/L (mildly elevated, consistent with hepatic metastases), Total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, Albumin 3.6 g/dL.
Coagulation: PT/INR within normal limits.
Tumor markers: CEA 61.3 ng/mL (down from 94.7 ng/mL at progression, and down from 31.2 ng/mL pre-progression baseline - overall trending down from the progressive disease nadir). CA 19-9: 198 U/mL (down from 287 U/mL at progression).
Urinalysis: No proteinuria (important given ramucirumab monitoring - protein/creatinine ratio checked 0.18, within acceptable range).

---

IMAGING RESULTS
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, reviewed today:

The CT imaging from this restaging shows encouraging early response to FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. All three bilateral pulmonary nodules have slightly decreased in size compared with the progression scan: the right upper lobe nodule is down to 7 mm (from 8 mm), the right lower lobe nodule is 5 mm (from 6 mm), and the left upper lobe nodule is 6 mm (from 7 mm). While these are modest reductions that may partly reflect measurement variability, taken together with the hepatic findings and falling tumor markers, they appear to reflect a genuine treatment response rather than measurement artifact.

The hepatic lesions have more clearly decreased: the dominant segment VI lesion is now 2.1 x 1.8 cm, down from 2.4 x 2.1 cm at progression. The segment II lesion has come down to 1.0 x 0.8 cm from 1.2 x 1.0 cm, and the segment IV lesion is 1.2 x 0.9 cm, down from 1.4 x 1.1 cm. The segment VII lesion which had reached near-complete response during first-line therapy is still not measurable. The previously noted perihepatic lymphadenopathy has slightly decreased as well (now approximately 1.0 cm). The primary sigmoid mass measures 2.8 x 2.2 cm, slightly smaller than the 3.1 x 2.5 cm measured at progression. No new hepatic lesions. No new pulmonary nodules. No peritoneal disease. No adrenal lesions.

Overall, this represents a borderline partial response - roughly 24% decrease in the sum of the target hepatic lesions from the progression nadir - with pulmonary nodules following a parallel trajectory. No new sites of disease. This is an encouraging early read for a second-line regimen in KRAS-mutant MSS colorectal cancer, where response rates are in the range of 20-25% and where disease control is the primary realistic goal.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 59-year-old male with Stage IV KRAS G12D-mutant, BRAF wild-type, MSS sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with bilobar hepatic metastases and pulmonary metastases (confirmed metastatic CRC on lung biopsy). He is 13 months out from initial diagnosis and is currently receiving second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab at modified irinotecan dosing (135 mg/m2, UGT1A1 *1/*28 heterozygous).

1. METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER - EARLY PARTIAL RESPONSE TO SECOND-LINE FOLFIRI PLUS RAMUCIRUMAB
The restaging CT today shows encouraging early response - hepatic lesion shrinkage, modest pulmonary nodule decrease, and no new sites of disease. Tumor markers (CEA and CA 19-9) are both trending down meaningfully from the progression peak. This is consistent with an objective partial response to FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab, which is gratifying given the modest response rates typically seen in RAS-mutant MSS disease.

Plan: Continue FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab at current doses. Will proceed with cycle 5 today pending counts (ANC adequate at 1.8, counts acceptable). No dose modifications are indicated at this time. Will continue monitoring irinotecan-related toxicities closely. Next restaging CT planned in 8-10 weeks (approximately after cycles 7-8). Will continue monitoring CEA and CA 19-9 at each cycle visit.

I discussed with Mr. Kowalski and his wife that while this is encouraging, second-line therapy for KRAS-mutant metastatic colorectal cancer remains palliative in intent and response durability is typically measured in months. The goal is to maintain disease control as long as possible while preserving quality of life. He understands this and remains motivated to continue treatment.

2. TREATMENT-RELATED HYPERTENSION (GRADE 2-3, ANTI-VEGFR2/ANTI-VEGF RELATED)
Blood pressure today is 146/94 mmHg, which represents an improvement from the Grade 3 values seen at cycle 2 visit (168/108 mmHg). With the addition of hydrochlorothiazide, he is now borderline Grade 2. Nephrology has been co-managing and has been satisfied with his current antihypertensive regimen (lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, HCTZ 25 mg daily). Will continue monitoring at each visit. BP target less than 140/90 mmHg ideally; may need further optimization. Not a reason to hold ramucirumab at this time given improvement trend. Urine protein/creatinine ratio is 0.18, below the threshold of concern for ramucirumab-associated proteinuria.

3. IRINOTECAN-INDUCED DIARRHEA / UGT1A1 PHARMACOGENOMICS
Grade 1-2 diarrhea is manageable at current irinotecan dose of 135 mg/m2. Averages 2-3 loose stools per day above baseline on infusion weeks. Instructed on continued dietary modifications (low-residue diet during infusion weeks), maintained loperamide rescue protocol. Will not uptitrate irinotecan dose given prior Grade 2 toxicity and UGT1A1 heterozygosity; may dose reduce further if diarrhea escalates. Patient cautioned to call with any increase in stool frequency above 6 per day or if associated with fever, dehydration, or inability to tolerate oral intake.

4. RESIDUAL OXALIPLATIN-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY
Neuropathy has improved from Grade 2-3 at its nadir to Grade 1 currently. Mild fingertip numbness bilaterally, not functionally limiting. Duloxetine 60 mg daily ongoing - patient reports this has helped with symptom management. Will continue monitoring. No new neurologic concerns on today's exam. Not affecting his ability to receive FOLFIRI (irinotecan does not cause cumulative neuropathy).

5. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS
Metformin 1000 mg twice daily continued. No hypoglycemia reported. Glycemic management stable; will check HbA1c at next visit. Renal function stable (eGFR 74), metformin appropriate.

6. HYPERLIPIDEMIA
Atorvastatin continued. No dose changes needed. LFTs mildly elevated but attributable to hepatic metastases rather than statin toxicity; consistent with prior pattern.

7. KRAS G12D TARGETED THERAPY / CLINICAL TRIAL PLANNING
Patient remains interested in KRAS G12D-targeted investigational therapy. We discussed the current landscape: KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors are distinct from the KRAS G12C inhibitors (sotorasib, adagrasib) that are approved in lung cancer and show modest activity in colorectal cancer. KRAS G12D is the most common KRAS mutation in colorectal cancer and represents an active area of investigational effort. We are tracking his eligibility for available clinical trials and he is registered with an NCI-designated cancer center trial. However, given the current partial response to FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab, it is appropriate to continue the current effective regimen and preserve the clinical trial option for a future line of therapy. Patient agrees with this approach. We will revisit clinical trial options at the time of progression or intolerable toxicity.

8. GOALS OF CARE / SUPPORTIVE CARE
Patient and wife are engaged and well-informed. Patient understands the incurable nature of his disease and continues to desire aggressive, active treatment. Advance directive is completed and on file. He is coping reasonably well emotionally; his wife is his primary support person. Social work referral offered and declined at this time. Palliative care co-management discussed - patient willing to meet with the palliative care team in a supportive capacity for symptom management, not exclusively end-of-life planning; referral placed.

FOLLOW-UP: Return in 2 weeks for cycle 5 pre-treatment labs and FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab administration. Routine labs (CBC with differential, CMP, CEA, CA 19-9, urine protein/creatinine) drawn at each infusion visit. Next restaging CT in 8-10 weeks. Patient instructed on diarrhea precautions, febrile neutropenia precautions (given prior borderline counts), and blood pressure monitoring at home (will report any BP >160/100 mmHg).

Total face-to-face time with patient: approximately 45 minutes. Greater than 50% of visit time spent in counseling regarding clinical trial options, goals of care, and review of imaging findings.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Hematology/Medical Oncology
